content:
Of course. Here are 5 multiple choice questions on "Checking the Stochastic Model" in Time Series Analysis, progressing from easy to challenging.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "q1",
    "question": "What is the primary purpose of checking the residuals of a fitted time series model?",
    "options": ["To verify they resemble white noise", "To forecast future values", "To estimate the model parameters", "To identify the order of integration"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The core assumption is that a good model will have residuals that are uncorrelated, have constant mean (zero), and constant variance—the properties of white noise. Checking this validates the model's adequacy."
  },
  {
    "id": "q2",
    "question": "Which diagnostic tool is most directly used to check for remaining autocorrelation in the residuals of an ARIMA model?",
    "options": ["The Autocorrelation Function (ACF) plot", "A histogram of the residuals", "A time series plot of the original data", "The PACF of the original series"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The ACF plot of the residuals is the primary tool. If no significant autocorrelation bars fall outside the confidence intervals, it suggests the model has successfully captured the predictable structure, leaving behind only white noise."
  },
  {
    "id": "q3",
    "question": "The Ljung-Box test is applied to the residuals of a fitted model. A high p-value (e.g., p > 0.05) leads to what conclusion?",
    "options": ["Fail to reject the null hypothesis; the residuals show no significant autocorrelation.", "Reject the null hypothesis; the residuals are not white noise.", "The model is perfectly specified.", "The test is inconclusive and must be run again."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The null hypothesis (H₀) of the Ljung-Box test is that the data are independently distributed (i.e., no autocorrelation). A high p-value means we lack evidence to reject H₀, supporting the conclusion that the residuals are white noise."
  },
  {
    "id": "q4",
    "question": "A model's residuals pass the Ljung-Box test but show clear evidence of heteroskedasticity in a plot of residuals vs. fitted values. What is the most appropriate next step?",
    "options": ["Consider a model that accounts for changing variance, like ARCH/GARCH.", "Add more AR or MA terms to the existing model.", "Difference the series again to achieve stationarity.", "Conclude the model is adequate and proceed to forecast."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": The Ljung-Box test only checks for autocorrelation. Heteroskedasticity (non-constant variance) is a separate violation of the white noise assumption. A model like ARCH or GARCH explicitly models and accounts for time-varying volatility.
  },
  {
    "id": "q5",
    "question": "When performing residual diagnostics, why is it insufficient to only check the ACF? What other critical property must also be verified?",
    "options": ["The assumption of constant variance (homoskedasticity)", "The stationarity of the original time series", "The seasonality present in the data", "The mean of the original series being zero"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "White noise is defined by both a lack of autocorrelation *and* constant variance. Checking the ACF only addresses the first property. One must also use plots (e.g., residuals vs. time or vs. fitted values) and tests (e.g., Breusch-Pagan) to validate the homoskedasticity assumption."
  }
]
```
